Self-describing via `version`. Every templated answer is reproduced by src/engine/binary.ts (exact bigint: binary strings, sums of powers of two, integer information bounds ⌈log₂N⌉, base-3 weighing counts, Bachet weights, popcount/XOR — no floats); the few free-form questions are sourced and engine-checked by id. interviewerPrompt:
ROLE
You are a senior quantitative-research interviewer at a top trading desk (Jane Street / Citadel / IMC), running a live mock interview on BINARY & INFORMATION. Be professional, probing, and fair-but-pressured. You are interviewing one candidate, right now, on the single question below.

THE QUESTION (injected at runtime)
- Prompt: {{prompt}}
- Tier: {{tier}} (hard | harder | brutal — calibrate pressure and follow-up depth)
- Source: {{source}} (your context only; never read it aloud)

PROTOCOL
1. Ask the question once from {{prompt}}, then let the candidate drive. ONE question at a time — never surface follow-ups early.
2. Make them think ALOUD and NAME the structure before computing: is this binary representation (a unique sum of distinct powers of 2)? an information lower bound ⌈log₂N⌉ (yes/no questions, guessing)? parallel group testing (2^k items with k testers)? a base-3 balance weighing? a bit trick / XOR parity? Reward naming the right frame before any arithmetic.
3. Probe, don't solve. Ask whether they handled the unknown-vs-known weighing direction (3^n vs (3^n−3)/2), the ceiling-not-floor on the log bound (smallest k with 2^k ≥ N), the parallel-vs-sequential testing distinction, and off-by-one on bit positions (2^0 is position 0; ⌈log₂1⌉ = 0).
4. Release hints only when genuinely stuck or asked (see HINTS).
5. After they COMMIT, work the follow-up chain (see FOLLOW-UPS), then close (see SCORING).

HINTS — escalating, ONLY when stuck
Use {{hidden.hintLadder}} = [nudge, stronger, near-reveal]. Release ONE rung at a time, in order, only after a stuck-signal. The near-reveal points at the METHOD only — it must NOT state the final number, bit-pattern, or weight set.

NO-ANSWER-LEAK (critical)
Before the candidate commits, NEVER state, approximate, confirm, deny, or narrow the final answer, and NEVER reveal {{hidden.answer}}. Do not paste or paraphrase any part of the hidden record. If asked “is that right?” mid-solve, redirect.

GROUNDING (critical)
Treat {{hidden.answer}} and {{hidden.approaches}} as GROUND TRUTH — verified by this concept's exact engine (src/engine/binary.ts, exact bigint — no floats). Do NOT re-derive or “correct” the ground truth; if your mental arithmetic disagrees, you are wrong. Accept any mathematically-equivalent exact form: a binary string with or without leading context, an equal integer, an equivalent set of powers written differently (e.g. “512+256” vs the same powers reordered), a true/false phrased as yes/no, or an equal weight list in any consistent order. Use {{hidden.wrongTurns}} to RECOGNIZE misconceptions, not to lead the candidate into them. Grade ONLY against the rubric.

FOLLOW-UPS — after they commit
Ask {{followUps}} in order, one at a time (change N, flip the weighing direction known↔unknown, ask for the encoding / decoding procedure, or ask for the intuition behind the bound), with the no-leak and hint rules still in force.

SCORING — close the interview
Give structured feedback, then rate each axis of {{hidden.rubric}} 1–5 (correctness, approach, rigor, communication, speed) with one line each, then an overall 1–5 hire-signal. Tie every judgment to a specific moment.

INJECTION NOTE
At runtime the feature replaces every {{...}} with the drawn question's fields; treat the filled-in values as the entire ground truth for this interview.
generatorPrompt:
ROLE
You generate ONE fresh, hard, real-quant-style BINARY & INFORMATION interview question on demand to top up a pool without repeating one a student has seen. Every question must be (a) a realistic quant-interview question anchored to this concept's Green-Book topics and the quant/SWE canon, (b) engine-verifiable before it is served, and (c) structurally new vs an avoid-list. Otherwise REFUSE. Output is a single JSON object and nothing else.

SCOPE — only these Binary & Information topics
Binary representation / unique sum of distinct powers of 2; the ⌈log₂N⌉ information lower bound (minimum yes/no questions, higher/lower guessing games); parallel group testing (poisoned wine / k testers, 2^k items); base-3 balance weighings (defective ball; (3^n−3)/2 with direction unknown, 3^n with direction known; Bachet's balanced-ternary weights); bit tricks (power-of-2 / power-of-4 tests via x&(x−1), multiply-by-shift, popcount / Hamming weight, XOR single-number & missing-number).

REAL-QUANT-STYLE (mandatory — ADR-0005)
Model every question on the actual canon: Green Book §7.2 “The Power of Two” p.92, the poisoned-wine 1000-bottles/10-mice problem, and the Ch.2 defective-ball weighing p.4–5; plus LeetCode 136 (Single Number), 231 (Power of Two), 191 (Number of 1 Bits), 342 (Power of Four), 268 (Missing Number); the gold-rod (1,2,4) and 2-eggs/100-floors puzzles. It must read like something genuinely asked on a desk — never an arbitrary puzzle that merely happens to be engine-solvable.

PREFER TEMPLATES (first choice); free-form only as a fallback. Engine-backed forms (src/engine/binary.ts):
- tmpl-binary-repr {n}                          → toBinary
- tmpl-powers-subset {n}                        → powersOfTwo (join with +)
- tmpl-bits-needed {N}                          → bitsNeeded (⌈log₂N⌉)
- tmpl-weighings {N,directionKnown}             → weighingsForN
- tmpl-popcount {n}                             → popcount
- tmpl-power-of-two {n}                         → isPowerOfTwo
- tmpl-power-of-four {n}                        → isPowerOfFour
- tmpl-single-number {values}                   → xorAll
- tmpl-missing-number {values}                  → missingNumber
- tmpl-bachet-weights {maxMass}                 → bachetWeights (join with ,)
- tmpl-egg-drops {floors}                       → eggDrops
- tmpl-multiply-shift {x,k,minus}               → multiplyByShift (and the (x<<k)−x identity)
- tmpl-balanced-ternary {target,weights}        → balancedTernary
Emit a free-form question ONLY if no template fits — and it STILL must pass engine verification, with fingerprint “sem:<hash>”.

ENGINE-VERIFY-BEFORE-SERVE (mandatory — ADR-0005)
Output the exact data to reproduce the answer with src/engine/binary.ts so the live feature can RUN the engine and REJECT anything it cannot verify. engineCheck = { module:"src/engine/binary.ts", calls:[exact call(s)], answer:<exact string the engine returns> }. The engine is exact bigint (integers / binary strings / bit-patterns / a {n,d} rational only for the fair-coin expansion) — every graded answer is a clean integer, a binary string, a set of powers of 2, a true/false, or a weight list. State the conventions explicitly: ceiling not floor on the log bound (smallest k with 2^k ≥ N); the weighing direction known (3^n) vs unknown ((3^n−3)/2); parallel (not sequential) testing for the 2^k group-testing forms; bit position 0 = the 2^0 bit.

AVOID-LIST / NO-OVERLAP
You are given avoidList (the student's seen-set ∪ the global pool). Your fingerprint MUST NOT be in it. Fingerprint = “<templateId>:<normalized-params>” or “sem:<hash>”. Change structure/params until new, or REFUSE.

HINTS: exactly 3 rungs (nudge → stronger → near-reveal); the near-reveal gives METHOD ONLY and must not state the final number, bit-pattern, or weight set. FOLLOW-UPS: ≥1. hidden.answer MUST equal engineCheck.answer.

SELF-REJECTION: if you cannot produce a question that is real-quant-style + Green-Book-anchored, engine-verifiable, and structurally new, output exactly { "refusal": true, "reason": "<which fence failed>" }. An honest refusal beats an unverifiable or repeated question.

templates.title: Binary representation of an integer
templates.description: Write n big-endian in base 2; each set bit is a power of two summing to n.

templates.title: Distinct powers of two summing to n
templates.source: Green Book §7.2 p.92 (unique sum of powers of two, GB6); dossier S1.
templates.description: List the set bits of n as powers of two, largest first (the gold-rod / subset-sum form).

templates.title: Minimum yes/no questions = ⌈log₂N⌉
templates.source: Green Book §7.2 p.92 (information bound); dossier S2/S3/S4.
templates.description: Smallest k with 2^k ≥ N: each yes/no answer is one bit, so the worst case needs ⌈log₂N⌉.

templates.title: Minimum balance weighings (base-3 bound)
templates.source: Green Book Ch.2 p.4–5 (GB5, defective ball); dossier S5/S6/S9.
templates.description: A balance is ternary: 3^n cases (direction known) or (3^n−3)/2 (unknown); find the smallest n covering N.

templates.title: Hamming weight (number of set bits)
templates.source: Green Book §7.2 p.92 (Kernighan n&(n−1)); dossier S13 (LeetCode 191).
templates.description: Count the 1 bits of n by clearing the lowest set bit repeatedly.

templates.title: Is n a power of two? ((n&(n−1))==0)
templates.description: A power of two has one set bit: n>0 and n&(n−1)==0.

templates.title: Is n a power of four?
templates.source: Green Book §7.2 p.92 (bit tricks); dossier S16 (LeetCode 342).
templates.description: A power of two whose single set bit is in an even position (2^0, 2^2, …).

templates.title: Single Number (XOR all)
templates.source: Green Book §7.2 p.92 (XOR identity); dossier S11 (LeetCode 136).
templates.description: Every value paired except one; XOR all, paired duplicates cancel.

templates.title: Missing Number (XOR range with values)
templates.source: Green Book §7.2 p.92 (XOR synthesis); dossier S15 (LeetCode 268).
templates.description: XOR 0..n with the values; the unmatched index is the missing number.

templates.title: Bachet's weights (balanced ternary)
templates.source: Green Book Ch.2 (base-3 weighing); dossier S10 (MathWorld "Weighing").
templates.description: Two-pan weights with coefficients {−1,0,+1}: powers of 3 covering 1..maxMass.

templates.title: 2-egg, F-floor worst-case drops
templates.source: Green Book Ch.2 (search bounds); dossier S7 (2 eggs, 100 floors → 14).
templates.description: Smallest x with x(x+1)/2 ≥ floors (decreasing-step strategy, not binary search).

templates.title: Multiply via shift (and optional subtract)
templates.description: x·2^k is x<<k; subtract one copy of x for the ×7 form.

templates.title: Balanced-ternary weight placement
templates.source: Green Book Ch.2 (base-3 weighing); dossier S10 (balanced ternary).
templates.description: Signed coefficients {−1,0,+1} over descending powers of 3 summing to the target.

questions.prompt: On the desk we encode order IDs as raw bit-strings. Write the integer 100 in base 2 (big-endian, no leading zeros). What is its binary representation?
questions.source: Green Book §7.2 p.92 (binary representation, GB6: "1000 = 1111101000"); dossier S17.

approaches: Subtract the largest power of two ≤ n repeatedly, placing a 1 in each consumed position (greedy / repeated halving).
Repeatedly divide by 2 and read the remainders bottom-up, or AND/shift the value bit by bit.
wrongTurns: Reversing the bit order (little-endian) or including a spurious leading zero.
Miscounting a power of two (e.g. treating 2^6 as off by one).
hintLadder: Express n as a sum of distinct powers of two; each present power is a 1 bit.
Find the largest power of two that fits, mark its position, subtract, and repeat down to 1.
Lay out bit positions from the highest power of two down to 2^0 and mark which powers you actually used; read them high-to-low.

rubric.correctness: States the exact bit-pattern / count / boolean and justifies why it is correct against the binary/log/base-3 structure.
rubric.approach: Reaches for the right tool (binary representation, the ⌈log₂N⌉ information bound, group testing, base-3 weighing, or a bit trick) rather than brute force or guessing.
rubric.rigor: Checks edge cases (powers of two, ties, off-by-one in the bound), states the encoding/decoding scheme explicitly, and proves the bound is tight (achievability + impossibility).
rubric.communication: Thinks aloud, names the structure (representation vs information bound vs group testing vs base-3) before computing, and explains the decode step.
rubric.speed: Reaches the answer without flailing; brutal tier allows more synthesis time across multiple ideas.

followUps: What is 100 in hexadecimal, reading the binary in nibbles?
How would you recover n from this bit-string, and what does the lowest set bit tell you about divisibility by 2?

questions.prompt: A coin tray holds one weight of each power of two (1, 2, 4, 8, …). Using each at most once, which weights sum to exactly 7? List the distinct powers of two (largest first).

approaches: Greedily take the largest power of two not exceeding the remainder; subtract and repeat.
Write n in binary; each 1 bit names exactly one power of two in the subset.

Stopping early and leaving a non-zero remainder.

Repeatedly peel off the largest power of two that still fits and subtract it.
Convert n to binary; report one power of two per 1 bit, from the highest down.

Direction known ⇒ smallest n with 3^n ≥ N: multiply 1×3×3… until it reaches N.

Multiply 1 × 3 × 3 × … and stop when the product first reaches N; that many weighings.

questions.source: Green Book Ch.2 p.4–5 "Defective ball" (GB5, base-3 bound); dossier S5/S6/S9.

approaches: A balance gives a base-3 (ternary) answer per weighing, so n weighings distinguish at most 3^n outcomes.
Direction unknown ⇒ smallest n with (3^n − 3)/2 ≥ N, since each item could be heavy or light and you must avoid the degenerate all-same outcome.
wrongTurns: Treating the balance as binary (log base 2) instead of ternary (log base 3).
Using 3^n ≥ N (the known-direction bound) and under-counting when the direction is unknown.
hintLadder: How many distinct outcomes does a single weighing on a three-state balance produce?
With the direction unknown each candidate splits into heavy/light, so n weighings cover only (3^n − 3)/2 cases; find the smallest n covering N.
Compute (3^n − 3)/2 for n = 1,2,3,… and stop at the first value that reaches N.

rubric.correctness: States the exact bit-pattern / count / boolean and justifies why it is correct against the binary/log/base-3 structure.
rubric.approach: Reaches for the right tool (binary representation, the ⌈log₂N⌉ information bound, group testing, base-3 weighing, or a bit trick) rather than brute force or guessing.
rubric.rigor: Checks edge cases (powers of two, ties, off-by-one in the bound), states the encoding/decoding scheme explicitly, and proves the bound is tight (achievability + impossibility).
rubric.communication: Thinks aloud, names the structure (representation vs information bound vs group testing vs base-3) before computing, and explains the decode step.
rubric.speed: Reaches the answer without flailing; brutal tier allows more synthesis time across multiple ideas.

questions.prompt: In a risk bitmask the set bits flag active positions. How many 1 bits (the Hamming weight) does the integer 11 have in binary?
questions.source: Green Book §7.2 p.92 (bit tricks); dossier S13 (Number of 1 Bits, LeetCode 191).

approaches: Kernighan's trick: repeatedly clear the lowest set bit with n & (n−1) and count the iterations.
Convert to binary and count the 1 digits directly.
wrongTurns: Counting total bit-length instead of only the set bits.
Including leading zeros or miscounting a run of consecutive 1s.
hintLadder: You only care about how many bits are 1, not the value.
n & (n−1) clears exactly the lowest set bit; count how many times you can apply it before reaching 0.
Either write n in binary and tally the 1 digits, or loop n &= n−1 and count the iterations.

questions.prompt: A capacity field must be a power of two for alignment. Is 16 a power of two? Answer true or false and justify with a single bit operation.
questions.source: Green Book §7.2 p.92 (GB1: power-of-two test (x & (x−1))==0); dossier S12 (LeetCode 231).

approaches: A power of two has exactly one set bit, so n > 0 and (n & (n−1)) == 0 iff it is a power of two.
Equivalently, popcount(n) == 1.
wrongTurns: Forgetting the n > 0 guard (the test misfires on 0).
Confusing power-of-two with merely even.
hintLadder: Think about how many 1 bits a power of two has.
For a single-set-bit number, n−1 flips that bit to 0 and sets all lower bits, so n & (n−1) wipes out.
Check that n is positive and that n & (n−1) equals zero; that is the whole test.

questions.prompt: Every value in the stream [2, 2, 1] appears exactly twice except one, which appears once. In O(1) extra space, which value is the singleton?
questions.source: Green Book §7.2 p.92 (XOR identity); dossier S11 (Single Number, LeetCode 136).

approaches: XOR all the values: each duplicated pair cancels (x ⊕ x = 0), leaving the unique value.
XOR is commutative and associative with identity 0, so order does not matter.
wrongTurns: Sorting or hashing for O(n) memory instead of using the O(1) XOR fold.
Forgetting that x ⊕ x = 0 and x ⊕ 0 = x.
hintLadder: Look for an operation under which any value combined with itself vanishes.
XOR is its own inverse, so paired duplicates cancel; what survives the fold?
Fold XOR across the whole list; the duplicates annihilate and only the singleton remains.

questions.prompt: The array [3, 0, 1] contains all but one of the integers 0..3. Using only O(1) extra space, which integer is missing?
questions.source: Green Book §7.2 p.92 (XOR synthesis); dossier S15 (Missing Number, LeetCode 268).

approaches: XOR all indices 0..n with all the array values; every present number cancels and the missing one survives.
Alternatively n(n+1)/2 minus the array sum gives the same answer (exact arithmetic).
wrongTurns: XORing only the values and forgetting to XOR in the full index range 0..n.
Off-by-one on the upper bound n = array length.
hintLadder: You have the complete set 0..n on one side and all-but-one of it on the other.
XOR the two multisets together; matched values cancel pairwise.
Fold XOR over both 0..n and the array entries; the unmatched index is what is missing.

questions.prompt: You have 2 identical eggs and a 36-floor building; an egg breaks above some unknown safe floor (and survives at or below it). What is the minimum number of drops that guarantees finding the highest safe floor in the worst case?
questions.source: Green Book Ch.2 (search bounds); dossier S7 (2 eggs, 100 floors → 14, GeeksforGeeks).

approaches: With only 2 eggs you cannot binary-search; the first egg must step in a decreasing arithmetic pattern so total worst-case cost stays flat.
Find the smallest x with x(x+1)/2 ≥ 36; that x is the answer.
wrongTurns: Using ⌈log₂(floors)⌉ — that assumes unlimited eggs / true binary search.
Fixing a uniform step size instead of decreasing the step after each survival.
hintLadder: With one egg left you must go floor-by-floor, so the first egg cannot leap by a fixed jump.
If the first drop is at floor x and survives, you have x−1 drops left, so the next jump is x−1, then x−2, … — sum these.
Find the smallest x whose triangular number x(x+1)/2 reaches the floor count.

questions.prompt: Without using the multiplication operator, multiply 5 by 7 using only shifts and one subtraction. What is the result?
questions.source: Green Book §7.2 p.92 (GB2: multiply by 7 as (x<<3)−x); dossier S14 (shift tricks).

approaches: A left shift by k multiplies by 2^k: 5 << 3 = 5 · 8.
Then subtract one copy of 5: 5·8 − 5 = 5·7.
wrongTurns: Shifting by the wrong amount (k must satisfy 2^k = the multiplier, or the nearest power above for the subtract form).
Forgetting to subtract the extra copy of x after shifting.
hintLadder: Shifting left by k is the same as multiplying by 2^k.
Pick k so 2^k is just above the multiplier, then correct by subtracting copies of 5.
Compute 5 << 3 (that is 5·8) and subtract 5.

questions.prompt: Using Bachet weights 9, 3, 1 on a two-pan balance, you must measure a mass of 11. Give the placement of each weight from highest to lowest, where +1 = weight on the pan OPPOSITE the mass, −1 = weight on the SAME pan as the mass, 0 = weight unused, so that the signed sum of (placement × weight) equals 11.
questions.source: Green Book Ch.2 (base-3 weighing); dossier S10 (balanced ternary, Bachet's weights).

approaches: Greedily from the largest weight: assign +1 if it helps reduce a positive remainder, −1 if the remainder is negative, else 0; then subtract its signed contribution.
This is just the balanced-ternary expansion of the target over powers of 3.
wrongTurns: Using only {0,+1} (ordinary ternary) and being unable to represent the target.
Processing weights low-to-high instead of high-to-low and losing the greedy uniqueness.
hintLadder: Each weight has three roles: with the load, against the load, or off — a signed digit.
Work from the largest weight down, choosing the sign that pulls the running remainder toward zero.
It is the balanced-ternary digits of the target over the given powers of 3; assign each weight ±1 or 0 greedily from the top.

questions.prompt: Using Bachet weights 9, 3, 1 on a two-pan balance, you must measure a mass of 5. Give the placement of each weight from highest to lowest, where +1 = weight on the pan OPPOSITE the mass, −1 = weight on the SAME pan as the mass, 0 = weight unused, so that the signed sum of (placement × weight) equals 5.
questions.source: Green Book Ch.2 (base-3 weighing); dossier S10 (balanced ternary, Bachet's weights).

approaches: Greedily from the largest weight: assign +1 if it helps reduce a positive remainder, −1 if the remainder is negative, else 0; then subtract its signed contribution.
This is just the balanced-ternary expansion of the target over powers of 3.
wrongTurns: Using only {0,+1} (ordinary ternary) and being unable to represent the target.
Processing weights low-to-high instead of high-to-low and losing the greedy uniqueness.
hintLadder: Each weight has three roles: with the load, against the load, or off — a signed digit.
Work from the largest weight down, choosing the sign that pulls the running remainder toward zero.
It is the balanced-ternary digits of the target over the given powers of 3; assign each weight ±1 or 0 greedily from the top.

questions.prompt: You have 1000 bottles of wine and exactly one is poisoned. The poison kills any test animal in about 18 hours, and you have 20 hours before a party. You can use lab mice, each of which you can have sip from any subset of bottles. What is the minimum number of mice that guarantees you identify the poisoned bottle?
questions.source: Green Book §7.2 p.92 (GB3, poisonous wine: label 1000 in 10-bit binary); dossier S8 (Brainstellar #31).

approaches: Label the bottles 1..1000 in binary; mouse i drinks from every bottle whose bit i is 1, and the dead/alive pattern across the mice reads off the bottle index.
k mice cover up to 2^k bottles, so you need the smallest k with 2^k ≥ 1000.
wrongTurns: Thinking you need one mouse per bottle (linear) instead of a binary labeling.
Using ⌊log₂1000⌋ = 9 (2^9 = 512 < 1000) and being one mouse short.
hintLadder: Each mouse ends the experiment in one of two states — dead or alive — so it carries one bit.
Encode every bottle by which mice drink from it; you need enough mice so 2^(mice) ≥ 1000.
Double 1,2,4,… and count the doublings until you reach or pass 1000.

approaches: A balance gives a ternary outcome; with direction unknown, n weighings distinguish (3^n − 3)/2 candidates, and (3^3 − 3)/2 = 12 ≥ 12.

questions.prompt: Given the array [4, 1, 2, 1, 2] where every element appears twice except one, find the single element that appears once — using only constant extra space.
questions.source: Green Book §7.2 p.92 (XOR identity); dossier S11 (Single Number, LeetCode 136).

approaches: XOR all elements; paired duplicates cancel (x ⊕ x = 0), leaving the unique value.
XOR is associative/commutative with identity 0, so the order is irrelevant.
wrongTurns: Using a hash set (O(n) memory) instead of the O(1) XOR fold.
Forgetting x ⊕ x = 0.
hintLadder: Find an operation where a value combined with itself disappears.
XOR is its own inverse; duplicates cancel under it.
Fold XOR across the whole array; only the unpaired element survives.

questions.source: Green Book §7.2 p.92 (GB4, simulate probability p via binary expansion of p).

approaches: Write p = 1/3 in binary by repeatedly doubling the remainder: 1/3 = 0.010101… (period "01").
Toss the coin to generate bits; at the first index where your bit differs from the p-bit, win iff your bit is smaller — the expansion drives the comparison.
wrongTurns: Assuming 1/3 terminates in binary (it does not — it repeats).
Getting the repeating pattern backwards (0.1010… would be 2/3, not 1/3).
hintLadder: Find the binary expansion of 1/3 by the doubling algorithm (double, compare to 1, subtract).
The remainder cycles, producing a repeating binary block; identify the 2-bit period.
Lay out the repeating "01" block to 8 places after the point.
